text: The laparoscopic view reveals a large subserous fibroid located at the fundus of the uterus.
The surgeon injects vasopressin into the uterus to reduce blood flow, preparing for the removal of the large subserous fundal uterine fibroid.
The surgeon makes an oblique incision on the anterior surface of the large subserous fundal uterine fibroid.
The surgeon enucleates the large subserous fundal uterine fibroid, carefully separating it from the surrounding uterine tissue during the laparoscopic myomectomy.
The surgeon removes another small fibroid from the uterus, following the previous enucleation of a larger fibroid.
The surgeon sutures the inner layer of the uterine defect using a continuous technique with a barb suture known as V-lock, ensuring secure closure of the tissue.
The surgeon sutures the cirrhosa layer of the uterus using interrupted sutures, completing the repair of the surgical site.
The uterus is shown with completed suturing, displaying the repaired surface after fibroid removal.
The surgeon makes an incision on a subserous fibroid, exposing the fibroid tissue beneath the uterine serosa.
The surgeon enucleates the fibroid, carefully separating it from the surrounding uterine tissue.
The surgeon sutures the defect in the uterine wall using interrupted sutures, completing the repair of the surgical site.
A surgical specimen bag is introduced into the abdominal cavity, likely for the retrieval of enucleated fibroids.
The surgeon places the enucleated fibroids into a retrieval bag within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon performs morcellation of the fibroids within the containment bag, fragmenting the fibroid tissue for removal.
The surgeon applies anti-adhesion gel over the incisions, covering the sutured areas to prevent postoperative adhesions.
